name: Begonia masoniana 'Variegata'
origin: Cultivated selection (derived from Begonia masoniana, native to southern China and northern Vietnam)
aboutText: Begonia masoniana 'Variegata' is a cultivated selection derived from Begonia masoniana Irmsch., the 'Iron Cross Begonia' — a rhizomatous species associated with the humid forest habitats of southern China and northern Vietnam, grown in warm, shaded, humid conditions that replicate its native understory environment. The base species is already remarkable for its large, heavily bullate and hairy leaves bearing the prominent dark iron-cross marking at the centre of each blade. In the 'Variegata' form, irregular creamy-white variegation is added to this iron-cross pattern across the deep green to olive lamina, producing leaves of layered visual complexity that are immediately striking. The creeping rhizome habit remains unchanged. A plant that makes the already-dramatic iron cross begonia more complex still — sought after by collectors for its rarity within the masoniana lineage.

morphology.leafShape: Large, broadly cordate, asymmetrical, heavily bullate
morphology.variegation: Dark iron-cross marking plus irregular creamy-white variegation on green-olive lamina
morphology.growthHabit: Creeping rhizome at or just above substrate surface

propagation.notes: Most rhizomatous begonias root readily from leaf sections or petiole cuttings placed on moist substrate. Keep warm and humid until plantlets emerge at leaf margins.

careGuide.substrate: Light, well-draining mix: 50% perlite, 30% potting compost, 20% orchid bark. Begonias are susceptible to root rot — excellent drainage is essential.
careGuide.watering: Allow the top half of the substrate to dry between waterings. Water at the base — wet leaves invite fungal problems. Reduce in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70%. More tolerant of average indoor humidity than tropical aroids, but consistent moisture prevents crispy leaf margins.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced liquid fertiliser at quarter strength every 2–3 weeks during the growing season. Do not feed in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months in spring. Begonias like shallow, wide pots that suit rhizomatous growth. Don't bury the rhizome.

commonProblems.cause: Poor air circulation combined with high humidity
commonProblems.fix: Improve airflow; avoid wetting leaves; treat with a dilute bicarbonate of soda spray

commonProblems.cause: Overwatering, dead leaves left on plant, poor air circulation
commonProblems.fix: Remove dead foliage promptly; increase airflow; reduce watering
Move to a brighter position — begonias need good indirect light to maintain compact growth

items: Check for powdery mildew — a white dusty coating on leaf surfaces, especially in dry conditions; it spreads rapidly between plants
Inspect for mealybugs at leaf axils and the underside of stems where they hide in white cottony deposits
Rhizome begonias should show firm, healthy rhizome tissue — avoid plants with soft, discoloured rhizomes
Most begonias ship well as leaf cuttings — if buying a cutting, ensure it has a healthy petiole and no signs of rot at the cut end

fieldNotes.title: Cross Pattern, Now with Added Complication
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: Begonia masoniana is the Iron Cross Begonia — that pleasantly unfussy species with a rough, textured surface and the dark cross-shaped zone centred on each leaf blade. The variegated selection overlays cream or pale sectors across that already distinctive surface. Whether this improves upon the original is a matter of taste: the cross pattern is partially obscured, the overall effect is busier, and the care requirements become somewhat more demanding as variegated tissue is metabolically weaker. The original masoniana is a better houseplant. The variegated form is a more interesting collector specimen. Decide accordingly.
